# Refactoring Roadmap Toward OSS Building Blocks This document captures concrete migration plans for replacing bespoke helpers with established open-source libraries. Each section outlines the current implementation, the recommended dependency, phased tasks, testing strategy, and risks. ## 1. Hierarchical `.env` Loader → `dotenv-flow` - **Current state**: `packages/email-processor/src/config/env.ts` recursively walks parent directories, parses `.env` files, and tracks loaded keys. - **Target OSS**: [`dotenv-flow`](https://github.com/kerimdzhanov/dotenv-flow) (or `dotenv-cascade` if we prefer explicit overrides). - **Refactor steps**: 1. Install the package in the workspace root (`npm install dotenv-flow --workspace packages/email-processor`). 2. Replace custom `loadEnvironment`/`clearEnvironment` exports with thin wrappers around `dotenv-flow/config` and `dotenv-flow/dotenv-flow` APIs; add options for `silent` and `default_node_env`. 3. Update all call sites (`authorize-gmail`, tests, etc.) to rely on the new wrapper or to import `dotenv-flow` directly. 4. Remove manual `.env` discovery logic and associated tests; write regression tests asserting precedence rules. - **Testing**: unit tests covering variable override order, integration smoke test invoking `loadEnvironment` from nested directories. - **Risks / mitigations**: ensure Windows path behaviour matches expectations; document how `.env.local` or `.env.development.local` are handled. ## 2. Console/File Logger → `pino` Multi-Stream - **Current state**: `dual-logger.ts` buffers writes manually; `console-interceptor.ts` monkey-patches global `console`. - **Target OSS**: [`pino`](https://github.com/pinojs/pino) with [`pino-multi-stream`](https://github.com/pinojs/pino-multi-stream) or `pino.transport`. - **Refactor steps**: 1. Add `pino` (and the multi-stream helper) to the email processor workspace. 2. Create a logging factory module exporting configured transports (stdout + per-run file) and a no-op logger. 3. Update `test-pipeline.ts` and other CLI tools to use the factory; drop console monkey-patching in favour of per-run logger instances. 4. Retire `ConsoleInterceptor` and `DualLogger`, adjusting tests to assert logs via the pino destination (or using hookable streams). - **Testing**: CLI integration test ensuring both console and file outputs are produced; snapshot or golden logs for deterministic formats. - **Risks / mitigations**: pino defaults to JSON logs—decide on pretty-print vs structured output; confirm log file rotation strategy if files may grow large. ## 3. Argument Parsing & Repo Discovery → `commander` + `find-up` - **Current state**: `test-pipeline.ts` manually parses `process.argv` and walks up directories to locate the workspace root. - **Target OSS**: [`commander`](https://github.com/tj/commander.js) for CLI parsing, [`find-up`](https://github.com/sindresorhus/find-up) or [`pkg-up`](https://github.com/sindresorhus/pkg-up) for locating `package.json`. - **Refactor steps**: 1. Introduce a CLI module that registers commands/subcommands with commander, including validation, defaults, and auto-generated help. 2. Replace `findRepoRoot` with a utility that leverages `find-up` to discover the workspace root asynchronously. 3. Split CLI logic into subcommands (e.g., `single`, `batch`, `date-range`, `replay`) for maintainability. 4. Update scripts (`npm run test:pipeline`) to call the new entrypoint; delete redundant parsing helpers. - **Testing**: command-level tests using `commander`'s `.parseAsync` with mocked args; end-to-end test verifying help text and invalid argument handling. - **Risks / mitigations**: ensure commander output does not break existing automation that relies on stdout; document breaking changes in README. ## 4. OAuth Callback Server → `@google-cloud/local-auth` - **Current state**: `oauth-callback-server.ts` spins up a custom `http.createServer`, handles HTML responses, and implements manual cleanup. - **Target OSS**: [`@google-cloud/local-auth`](https://github.com/googleapis/google-auth-library-nodejs/tree/main/packages/local-auth) (built on `google-auth-library`). - **Refactor steps**: 1. Evaluate `local-auth` API support for Gmail scopes; alternatively, compose a lightweight Express server with `http-terminator` if `local-auth` is unsuitable. 2. Replace bespoke server creation in `authorize-gmail.ts` with the OSS helper, ensuring we still surface friendly CLI messages. 3. Remove embedded HTML templates; rely on built-in success/failure responses or serve static files if needed. 4. Update docs to reflect the new flow and dependencies. - **Testing**: integration test using supertest (if Express) or a mock HTTP client; manual smoke test walking through OAuth dance. - **Risks / mitigations**: confirm the helper library supports our desired redirect URIs; ensure we can still run headless authorization in CI (fall back to existing server if gaps appear). ## 5. Retry Logic → `p-retry` - **Current state**: `gmail-client.ts` implements exponential backoff with jitter, special-casing rate-limit errors. - **Target OSS**: [`p-retry`](https://github.com/sindresorhus/p-retry) (or `async-retry`). - **Refactor steps**: 1. Wrap Gmail API calls (`list`, `get`, etc.) inside `p-retry` with a predicate that inspects `error.code`/`reason`. 2. Expose retry options through configuration (max retries, factor, minTimeout) rather than duplicating them manually. 3. Delete custom `executeWithRetry` and `sleep`, ensuring tests cover both retry success and failure exhaustion. 4. Validate compatibility with `googleapis` promise semantics and error shapes. - **Testing**: unit tests stubbing operations to fail on first attempts and succeed later; timing-insensitive assertions by mocking timers. - **Risks / mitigations**: `p-retry` default aborts on `AbortError`; ensure Gmail errors are preserved with sufficient context; verify this does not interfere with request quotas. ## 6. Config Loader & Watcher → `cosmiconfig` + `chokidar` - **Current state**: `config-loader.ts` performs JSON parsing, hashing, caching, backups, and `fs.watch` hot reloads. - **Target OSS**: [`cosmiconfig`](https://github.com/davidtheclark/cosmiconfig) for discovery/loading, [`chokidar`](https://github.com/paulmillr/chokidar) for file watching (or adopt a config manager like `node-config` if multi-env needed). - **Refactor steps**: 1. Introduce a configuration service built atop cosmiconfig with custom loaders for JSON and schema validation (retain Zod). 2. Replace manual hashing/caching with cosmiconfig's cache or a simpler memoization keyed by file mtime. 3. Swap `fs.watch` with chokidar to handle network filesystems and cross-platform edge cases; emit consistent events for reloads. 4. Revisit backup/rotation logic—either rely on VCS history or reimplement minimal snapshotting using `tempy`/`fs-extra`. 5. Simplify `ConfigurationLoader` API to focus on validation and stage-specific compilation. - **Testing**: unit tests covering cache invalidation, watcher events (use chokidar's `awaitWriteFinish`), and error propagation. - **Risks / mitigations**: ensure chokidar does not introduce performance overhead; evaluate memory usage if keeping compiled artifacts in cache. ## 7. HTML Sanitization → `html-to-text` - **Current state**: `template-utils.ts` strips HTML with regexes, which can fail on malformed markup and does not escape scripts robustly. - **Target OSS**: [`html-to-text`](https://github.com/html-to-text/node-html-to-text) or `sanitize-html` depending on the desired output. - **Refactor steps**: 1. Install the chosen sanitizer and configure it for Gmail-like HTML (link handling, whitespace preservation). 2. Replace regex-based sanitization in `getEmailBodyText` with the library call; expose configuration for future tuning. 3. Adjust tests to assert on sanitized output, adding fixtures with complex HTML (inline styles, malformed tags, scripts). 4. Remove manual regex helpers to eliminate duplication (also check `email-utils.ts`). - **Testing**: golden-output tests comparing sanitized body text, including XSS-prone inputs; performance benchmark for large emails. - **Risks / mitigations**: library default options may strip content we rely on (e.g., `
` vs newlines)—tune options and document differences. ## 8. Container Detection → `is-inside-container` - **Current state**: `email-query.ts` checks `/.dockerenv`/`/run/.containerenv` manually to choose the Postgres host. - **Target OSS**: [`is-inside-container`](https://github.com/sindresorhus/is-inside-container) (or `is-docker`). - **Refactor steps**: 1. Replace `detectPostgresHost` logic with the library helper, keeping the ability to override via `POSTGRES_HOST`. 2. Add a small abstraction that accepts overrides and falls back gracefully. 3. Update unit tests to mock the helper rather than relying on filesystem state. - **Testing**: unit test verifying host selection inside/outside container contexts by stubbing the helper. - **Risks / mitigations**: ensure library detection works in our CI/container runtime (Kubernetes, Docker Compose); keep fallback for unexpected environments. --- **Next Actions** 1. Prioritize replacements based on maintenance pain (env loading/logging are likely quick wins). 2. For each section, open a focused ticket capturing timeline, owner, and acceptance criteria. 3. Track migrations in the README/CHANGELOG to communicate new dependencies and behaviour changes.
